posts.author: gsapte
posts.text: This is logical observation, hence my investment thesis for HDFC LIFE is not for long term but a short to mid term opportunity. It trades at lower valuations due to low growth across multiple time intervals and spikes by 30% to 50% when overall insurance sector looks positive. That is typically the gain which an investor can benefit. For long term compounding, Insurance sector may not be a good candidate in India due to erratic and rampant regulations, sudden changes in GST slabs, no innovation in products. Your point that, with such low Per Capita GDP, expecting TAM of 100 Cr population (as p…

posts.author: Atul_Desai
posts.text: Life insurance firms do well when there is more affluence. When the GDP p capital.is as low as india , and so many are poor it’s not attractive . It should be the other way around, unfortunately. The TAM is just 40 to 50 m people in india . People get confused by population and per capital insurance coverage being low and compare it to China or middle / rich nations. Seperately , there is a lot to be said about the management of HDFC LIFE as well of HDFC in general. They have lost the mojo that Aditya Puri and Deepak Parekh had, and have been rather sad in their management leadership and focus…

posts.author: Investor_No_1
posts.text: Agree, however i dont read too much into what they had to say a decade back or now, I see that market sees penetration as percent of people insured in a community….i see it as the quality & quantum with which each person out of them is insured….and there is huge huge scope in improvement of that maybe not just in india but even globally….here comes product mix & innovation…..next thing is adjacencies and licence to cross sell maybe health products….also annuity/pension in a country with very little social security is another thing…… So opportunity is undoubtedly huge, to say the least….but wha…

posts.author: gsapte
posts.text: This is correct understanding. Analysts and Media keep saying that, India is under penetrated in both Life Insurance and Non Life Insurance, and hence all stocks in this category are long term compounding businesses. They may be partially correct. But investment is not as simple as it sounds. P/B of many insurance companies in India is still well above International valuations. In Developed Markets, insurance company does not trade at P/B > 2.0 but here these companies trade at very high valuations sighting long growth ahead but that limits the upside in stock price. Banks on the other hands a…

posts.text: well thats a good strategy even for consisten compounders stalwarts as explained beautifully by peter lynch…I could have done that easily over last probably 7-8 years i hold this stock but as per my strategy i had been biying in big dips and crashes so whenever it recovers, the paper cagr is good…off late i have been avoiding the buying more..one reason could be i feel my asset allocation to life insurance at around 7% of portfolio is big enough (when i started constructing portfolio i had made a target of at least 10% but now feel a 5% allocation is also just fine for me) and other reason cou…

posts.author: gsapte
posts.text: Yes, both of you are right. HDFC LIFE is not a Buy and Hold Stock. It corrects quite sharply from time to time. My thesis of investment has been Enter it when it looks very undervalued and Exit once it starts looking fairly valued / overvalued. Mostly I decide this based on its own MCap / Embedded Value. This criteria is sufficient to generate 30%-35%-40% kind of returns in a reasonable time frame. Disc: Invested again at current valuations in the past 2-3 months over a period. I invest in Insurance business mainly to ensure that, they give BFSI exposure, but do not have NPA(s) in my BFSI port…

posts.text: Unfortunately that has been the case since listing. Reason may also be insane valuations at which it listed. Question is for how long will it not remain a compounding machine and keep paying price of changing rules and higher valuation? btw life insurance is one of very few sectors where both FII and DII have been consistently raising stake since last 5-10 years. posts.author: gsapte
posts.text: You can either check it manually, by using web site : HDFC Life (HDFCLIFE.NS) - Market capitalization and then use the latest Q4 FY26 Presentation Slide 6, where last 5-8 year Embedded Value is mentioned. Alternatively, you can use AI tools and they generally give reasonably accurate data the for past 5-8 years. HDFC LIFE has traded at MCap / Embedded Value of 5.0 after listing and again in FY25, the ratio went up taking the stock price to 820+ and Now it is trading at 550 close to 2.0 (MCap/EV). This stock is not a compounding machine as Life Insurance Sector is regulated in India and regulat…

posts.author: gsapte
posts.text: After Q4 FY26 results, following is the situation: Embedded value: INR 62,139 cr ; Operating RoEV 15.0% (15.4% normalized). VNB: INR 4,034 cr , +2% YoY . Total (EV+VNB) : 62,139 + 4,034 Cr = Rs. 66,173 Cr Current Market Cap : 1,20,423 Cr Current MCap / Total EV = 1,20,423 / 66,173 = 1.8. This is below its Historical Avg of 2.5. Due to sluggish VNB growth, and Lower growth in various parameters, Stock seems to have corrected from 800+ to 550 as of today. MCap/EV of 1.8 or 1.9 looks quite reasonable or undervalued for quality business like HDFC LIFE. May be, there is generally some pressure of H…
